author: ben_a_adams
content: What criteria would a theme of AA be judged on to be a success vs failure, or partial win?

author: ben_a_adams
content:
i.e. what are the goals; what is trying to be achieved

Otherwise is sloppy and could do anything and say AA was a great success even if what was done isn't very useful

author: ben_a_adams
content: Unless the theme of AA is "do frames" which then cheating a bit and starting with solution without clearly stating what its trying to solve

author: .fjl
content: The list you provided is pretty accurate I would say. One thing missing is the 'withdrawal from privacy pool' use case. For this, an account supplies a 'note' to withdraw from a pool, and then uses said note to pay for the gas of the transaction.

author: .fjl
content: To judge the success, we could define a subset of the list and then declare this 'essential'. But it's not really necessary of we find a solution that achieves all.

author: .fjl
content: We do have to define a subset of supported AA operations for the mempool. In Matt's document, we outlined the three strategies, where strat 2 has the canonical paymaster. We arrived at the idea of the canonical paymaster as a compromise between allowing no sponsoring, or arbitrary sponsoring scheme. The canonical paymaster is enough for a lot of use cases and also mempool safe.

author: milos88
content: And FOCIL and VOPS compatibile as well, right?

author: .fjl
content: Yes. Although the VOPS compatibility is heavily challenged. It will be basically impossible to define VOPS when a transaction can access the code of any account and also the sender storage.

author: milos88
content: I was referring to canonical paymaster only. But yes, there are other aspects of mempool rules that are not compatible with VOPS
